speaker: interviewee
text: At the same time. But I was sent to the forced labor camp. And they went to become soldiers. Is anybody there?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, he's just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, there's someone in a car out there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just hang on. Don't hop up. I'll just tell to come in here. It's a Subaru, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is your son's car a Subaru?

speaker: interviewee
text: Andrew?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it-- the car he drives, is it--

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll do the ID?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 2. The interviewee is Mr. George Meles. And it's January 9, 1995. Before you were sent to the work camp, did they give you any instructions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you the instructions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it came officially in a paper. They said, well, you have to present yourself in Beresk on October the 1st. And you have to buy your own clothing. And they are going to supply nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it means that we have bought my own clothing plus a sleeping bag. The sleeping bag which was very important, because it saved my life. I was using it for the next-- next 20 months. I was sleeping in it day and night. And this and my will to-- to live was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the reason why I survived the Holocaust. Did your mother or father say anything to you before you went to work camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they-- they-- they have just the-- they have bought and whatever I needed. And at that time, we could receive parcels. And I received food parcels. And if I would have needed another piece of clothing, they could have sent it to me. It went on for about half a year until March 19 when the Germans have occupied Hungary. Occupation wasn't really an occupation. They just came in and they said, and from now on, we are in charge. And from then-- that point, it wasn't any fun for the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were able to-- you were able to receive mail and parcels. Were there people able to visit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had-- they had-- they had the right to visit me. My mother visited me one month and my fiancee visited me the other month. And it was a longer ride for them. But of course, it was very important for both of them. And I don't know why. And-- and they have brought me whatever I needed. We were writing letters to each other every second day, and like with my parents and with my fiancee, we have communicated very well. And it was, actually, in those days somehow in-- in '43, somehow the Hungarian anti-Jewish laws were relaxed. It means that the Jews weren't persecuted openly. And they weren't really-- they weren't really murdered. It was just more or less a normal life. The only reason we felt [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bad because there was still discrimination between soldiers and-- and-- and forced labors-- forced laborers. Now, it was a very sudden-- I've mentioned before that we were digging holes, which was futile. But we were also trained. Now, when the regular soldier had a gun, and he was exercising with a gun, we were-- we had received a spade from the Hungarian government. And we were exercising with a spade. The spade looks like a gun. So they wanted us to go through the routine exercise as if you would do it with a gun. Now, I-- I will say, in those days, the Hungarian military wasn't with forced laborers. They weren't really very, very, very bad. They were treating us fairly well, especially they were treating me fairly well, because as I told you before, I never wanted to rock the boat. I behaved. I was never arguing with anybody. I was never doing anything against the rules. Now, other boys, other forced laborers, we were at that time 21 years old. And we had another group who was 22 years old. We were doing our job. But certain kids, they were doing-- they went out in the night. And they didn't come back until late. And then they were caught by the gentile soldiers, because there were gentile soldiers who were ordering us around. And they were caught. Then they were punished. The punishment was quite a painful punishment. They have [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] tied of the hands of the boys together around here with a rope. And then they were pulled up on a three or something like that in such a manner that they their toes couldn't reach the ground. And it means that all of your weight was on your wrist. And they were there one or two hours. There were some boys who were-- who have fainted. When they were faint-- when they have fainted, then they were-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Uh-oh. Then they were-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] They-- there was-- cold water was thrown over them. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- They were revived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: They were revived.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is not going to stop because they don't hear it in the back. It has to be-- can you stop it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I come back to the letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't do the letter, no?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we'll come back to it. Do you need your glasses for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you need the glasses?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to read the letter, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we should just get them ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to read it now? You want me to put them on?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to talk--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't need it if I talk--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you could put them on just before you read the letter. But we're going to pick it up again from--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The boys were punished in such a manner that their hands were tied together at the wrist. And then they were pulled up on-- on a tree or anything high above until their toes did not reach the ground anymore. So it means that all their weight was supported by the bound wrist. Now, it usually, it went on for about two hours. There was always somebody there to watch them. And when they went limp, then there was water, which they were-- they were throwing cold water on them so they would survive. And if they couldn't survive, then they were taken off. And they were down on the ground for about 20-- 20 minutes or half an hour. And then they hung them again. So it was really a very severe punishment. And their-- their wrists were swollen for-- for a week or even longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we got enough food. And we got food parcels from our parents. And we were in bunk beds. So there was straw in the bunk beds. And at that time, we didn't have any lice. It was clean. And the only thing is that we couldn't take a bath. We had cold water to wash our hands and-- and faces. But if you had soap from home, then you could use soap. If you haven't, then you just had the cold water. Now, there was an incident with about 200, 250 in one concrete building. And there was only, I would say, about a few toilets-- latrines. They were built of wood. So there was-- it was-- it was like a regular latrine with roof and side and back and doors. But it wasn't enough. So when some of the people wanted to do the thing, then it wasn't enough. And some people were just going behind built-up latrines. And they were, should I say, urinating there. And it went on for a week or two. And then there was an officer in charge who realized it was going on. And we were punished in such a manner that we had to crawl through that urinated mud. And when it came to the-- the softer part. And then he stepped on our head so our nose went right into it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was [LAUGHS] some kind of punishment, which wasn't very humane. Otherwise, we just didn't feel any worse than-- than-- than other people. So we had living accommodation under solid roof-- under solid roof. And we were-- we had enough food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had our exercise with a spade. And we-- I-- we couldn't really complain. As a Jew in 1943 and 1944, I didn't have much to complain about. Now, later on, we have been taken about early January, we were taken from Transylvania to-- to the northern part of Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you left, were there any letters that you received?

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't received letters regularly. The letters came until-- until June. In June, as you know, my wife-- my fiancee and my parents were taken away to Auschwitz. And that we have the last letters here. But before that, I have gone to northern Hungary in the Carpathian Mountains of [INAUDIBLE]. And we went to [NON-ENGLISH], and then later to [NON-ENGLISH]. Our job was-- and this made some sense-- we were cutting pine trees. And we were carrying the pine trees to the top of the mountains. Now, it was interesting how we couldn't cut any pine tree. We were told which parcel of pine tree can be cut down. And we found it out these were the part of the forest which was owned by local Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jews were very upset because once the trees were cut out of the property, they had nothing, just their land. But they didn't have to worry about it because they were deported to Auschwitz in about a month's time. And whoever survived and came back didn't need the forest anyway because the-- the communists have taken it over. So it was avoided for nothing. How did you live? What were your living quarters like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were living in private homes. Now, those homes belonged to local peasants. They were Ukrainians. They called it [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means this side of the Carpathians, not [NON-ENGLISH], the other side, which faced Poland at that time, which was also Ukrainian territory. Now, we have-- we have been living there. And it was a huge, big room where the whole family lived. Now, they usually had two or three children. They were living at one part of room. And there were the goats. The goats were in the same room. The only difference is they were in one side of the room. And they were living in another side room. And we as forced laborers, we were living on top of the-- of-- of the wire-- wire top of the-- where the goats were fenced in and had the wire top. And if you would ask me today if the goat smelled or we smelled, I couldn't tell the difference because the goats-- the minute we have come home, we were so dead tired that we went to sleep immediately. Now, I have to tell you about these trees-- tree trunks, which were cleaned of the branches. We had the one man in the front, the other one in the back. And if you're lucky, then they put in another two men. It depends how heavy the tree trunks were. And we started to carry those tree trunks in the morning. And in the afternoon, we reached the top where the trees were supposed to be needed. When we moved from here-- from-- from this-- from [NON-ENGISH] to [NON-ENGLISH], then it changed a little bit, the situation. We were also living at the different private homes. But we had a private room with other goats. We missed the goats because they were warm. And with other goats, the room was cold. We have--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was wintertime by this? It was wintertime?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the wintertime. Now, we had then-- we could take some tree trunks home-- not the whole ones, just pieces-- which we were supposed to cut up, and there was a stove which we could burn the tree trunks or whatever would we could get. And but many times, we were so tired that we just went to sleep and covered myself in my [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sleeping bag and went to sleep immediately. And we went to sleep around 7 o'clock or 8 o'clock. And at 6 o'clock, we got up. But the night was so short that we didn't realize that the night was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were living with the locals, were people trying to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no-- we could escape. Escape where? There was if-- the only place we could escape was going back to our parents. And if we tried to do that, they would have come and got us. There weren't people who were escaping, even after when our life was depending on it, very few people escaped because if they were found, they were shot dead right there and then. So it was not much of a help. Now, I have to tell you that at that time when we had enough tree trunks on-- on top of a mountain, we were digging holes-- squares. It's as big into the ground as a room. Now, when it was done properly, we-- we have put in the tree trunks. And we lined it from inside, and at the bottom, and at the top. Now, this was supposed to be a-- a bunker, a fortification, if and when the Russians are going to come. I use the expression Russians because that's what we were using at that time, even though it was the Soviet Union which one contained Tajikistan, and Krygis, and all sorts of the other Tartars. But we just referred to them as the Russians. We were doing a very proper job. And it was done well. And when the-- when the spring came, the ground melted. And the hole-- hole in the ground was full of water and our beautiful work went up in smoke because the-- these tree trunks were swimming on top of the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't really do much for the water fjord of the German-Hungarian armies. After we were there for about, I would say, about two months or so. And towards the end of March, we were-- they had decided that our group is going to go over to Poland and have the Hungarians in their water fjord. As we were going up to mountain, they were-- the Hungarians were fighting the Russians with horse-drawn carts. There was a-- there were two horses. And the cart was full of ammunition or whatnot. They were not motorized. They were horse-drawn carts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, since these roads were very steep at certain places, we were told that we have to go, two of us, to each and every cart. And we were supposed to push and have the horses up the mountain. [LAUGHS] I don't wonder that the Hungarians and the Germans lost the war, because with this equipment, you don't really go very far. By that-- this time, the Russians were very close to the Polish-Hungarian border. They were past Kiev. And they were very close to Stanislau. Stanislau is the-- is the place where we were heading. But before we went through the Polish-Hungarian border, we were told that every-- every clothing, every equipmen, anything what we had, and they think we cannot carry it with us, should be put it in one house. And we did that. And of course, then the Hungarians when we left were looking after. And whatever they wanted, they could take. And we were just walked away with-- with very, very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you receive-- when did you receive the last written communication?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last written communication we received on June 25th, which was sent to me by my parents and my fiancee from the ghetto. Now, let me read my father's letter in which he said, 'We are sending you two of our photographs' two photographs of ourselves and 'We are well. We are thinking a lot of you. And Ebbe,' who is my fiancee, 'is talking about you all the time. She behaves very well.' He writes again, 'We are well.' And I shouldn't worry about them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And whether the-- the room in the ghetto-- they didn't say ghetto but, 'Where we live is fairly good.' And write about you. And by that time, I was in the hospital. So this was written in June, June the 25th. And I got into the hospital on June the 6th on-- on-- on the Normandy-- on the same day. And now, my mother said exactly the same. And she says, 'We are just thinking [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] of you all the time. And we are talking with your father and with your fiancee about you.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and she is a meeting with other people. And my mother wasn't really-- didn't have too many friends or acquaintances. And I think I'm going to drink some water someplace, which I don't find. OK, that's-- what did I do with it? Nothing. So anyway, then my-- my-- my fiancee was writing a regular letter and said, we won't be able to send you any more letters. This is our last letter-- letter. And she admonishes me that I should look after myself. Of course, I was looking after myself as much as I could. And at that time, she was still believing in God. And she hoped that God is going to help me and-- and said I shouldn't despair-- writes about-- about God, who was just about ready to kill them-- killed-- killed her parents and my parents. So it's a good advice what I have received from her. And admonishes me that I should watch out for myself seemingly. She didn't really think that God is going to help me. And she sends me kisses. And she loves me forever. Now, it's beautiful. Of course, God didn't help me. And God didn't help her and my parents either. God was on the other side. So what happens is before all this, we have-- as I told you, we were crossing over to Poland. But before we have crossed over, or the officer who was in charge, was stopping us on the-- on the border as Jews. And we had to sing the Hungarian hymn and then some other patriotic songs. And then we stepped out of Hungary into Poland. How did you travel?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: We were walking all the time. We were walking up the Carpathians. And on the other side, we were walking down the Carpathians. I forgot to tell you that at that time, the food became scarce. And as we were helping those horse-drawn carts, one of the horses broke his leg or something happened. He was destroyed. And then I went in-- I and other boys went there. And we cut off from the thigh of the horse meat which wasn't a very easy job, because the horse had thick skin. But anyway, we did it, because by that time, we started to feel hungry. And when we had an opportunity at the next stop, we were putting it on-- on-- on a stick. And we ate it as a shish kebab. Of course, it was only horse meat. And there was no onion or-- or any other ingredient, which is necessary to make a shish kebab. I never had seen shish kebab before I came to Canada. So I didn't know that the situation was such an extraordinary thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a march was it to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the march was, I would say, about 150 kilometers, and it has taken quite a long time because we were, as I told you, we were helping the horses to cross over the-- the mountains or go up the mountains. It's interesting. We just push them up. They could come down without any help. And that was the first time when we went over to Poland. And we were in-- we were over-nighting in a house that we have-- we got lice. The reason we got the lice is because there were other soldiers or other forced laborers or somebody-- a group of people who had lice. So when we went to sleep, we were clean. When we got up in the morning, we were full of lice. And we were full of lice for a long, long time. There was just no way to get rid of it. Now, our aim was to get to Stanislau. Stanislau was a major Polish in that neighborhood. And they were storing our some things. They were storing food for animals. They were storing food for people. With my luck, I got to the place where they were storing the food for horses. And I was trying to eat the fodder. But it didn't agree with me. So I was just-- I just abandoned it. So I was eating one or two beets. And then I decided this is for the horses. But other soldiers, forced laborers, who got into another-- another part of that storage got real food for human beings. And they broke one or two. And they ate what was in it. Of course, we were told not to do it. But they did it just the same. Now, when were doing it during the day, for a day or two, and then we had to carry ammunition for the-- for the soldiers who were fighting the front line. It was very interesting to go. We were taken by the time by horse and-- and-- and carried. And also, we had some kind of a truck, one or two. And we were riding it because we put it on the truck. And then we arrived to the front-- front line. We had to take it off and put it someplace wherever they could use it up. Now, I never had been to the front line before. I had never been after. It was like-- it was during the night. And there were all sorts of-- it was like a firework they were shooting. And I--
